romania.title: Exporturile americane de GNL către Europa au scăzut sub 50% pentru prima dată în aproape doi ani
romania.summary: Exporturile de gaz natural lichefiat (GNL) ale Statelor Unite către Europa au scăzut semnificativ în luna iunie, deoarece preţurile mai ridicate din Asia şi importurile record ale Egiptului au determinat traderii să redirecţioneze transporturile către pieţe mai profitabile, transmite Reuters.

romania.title: Val de căldură record în Anglia: temperaturi fără precedent și cea mai fierbinte lună iunie din istorie
romania.summary: Anglia a cunoscut cea mai călduroasă lună iunie din istoria înregistrărilor sale, cu mai multe zile marcate de temperaturi record, a anunţat miercuri agenţia britanică de meteorologie Met Office.

romania.title: Val de căldură istoric în Statele Unite. Autoritățile avertizează că pot fi doborâte recorduri de temperatură
romania.summary: O mare parte din teritoriul Statelor Unite se confruntă începând de miercuri cu o căldură sufocantă, care ar putea să doboare mai multe recorduri de temperatură în zilele următoare şi să afecteze totodată Cupa Mondială de fotbal şi festivităţile ocazionate de cea de-a 250-a aniversare a acestei ţări.

romania.title: Școala de vară BookLand își deschide porțile în Campusul din Vulturești
romania.summary: În perioada 06 iulie – 04 septembrie 2026, Asociația BookLand organizează prima ediție a programului „Școala de vară BookLand”. De luni până vineri, zilnic între orele 09:00 și 16:00, copiii… Articolul Școala de vară BookLand își deschide porțile în Campusul din Vulturești apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Organizația Națională Cercetaşii României – Centru Local “Cetatea Băniei”, Craiova, vă invită sâmbătă, 4 iulie 2026, incepând cu ora 17:00, în Parcul Nicolae Romanescu, Zona Foișorului Fanfarei, la o nouă… Articolul Craiova: Redescoperă frumusețea lucrurilor mărunte la cea dea IX-a ediția a Festivalului Luminii, în Parcul Nicolae Romanescu apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Ediția 33 se deschide cu “Royal Jazz Night” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: OAR a inaugurat pavilionul Sides of Interconnected Learning la Congresul Mondial al Arhitecților UIA 2026 Barcelona. Devenind interdependenți: arhitectura românească în dialog cu lumea
romania.summary: În perioada 29 iunie – 2 iulie 2026, Ordinul Arhitecților din România (OAR) participă la cea de-a XXIX-a ediție a Congresului a Congresului Mondial al Arhitecților organizat de Uniunea Internațională a Arhitecților (UIA), desfășurat la Barcelona, Capitală Mondială a Arhitecturii 2026. Prin prezența sa în cadrul expoziției congresului găzduite de Muzeul de design /… Articolul OAR a inaugurat pavilionul Sides of Interconnected Learning la Congresul Mondial al Arhitecților UIA 2026 Barcelona. Devenind interdependenți: arhitectura românească în dialog cu lumea apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Nicuşor Dan a fost văzut azi la un grup şcolar din Buzău, unde surse politice spun că ar fi dat examenul de Bacalaureat la matematică în locul lui Marcel Ciolacu. „Da, poate l-am ajutat pe Ciolacu dându-mă drept el la Bac, […] The post Omul PSD-ului până la capăt: Nicuşor Dan a dat azi Bacul la mate în locul lui Ciolacu appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Fostul fotbalist, actual drojdier, Gabriel Tamaş acuză o mare nedreptate în noua lege a stadioanelor! Potrivit lui Tamaş, deşi e un lucru bun că berea va fi în sfârşit permisă la meciuri, e „absolut revoltător” că de aceasta se vor putea bucura doar suporterii din galerie, nu şi jucătorii de pe teren. „După ce că […] The post Tamaș, revoltat de legea care permite berea pe stadion: „Pot să bea doar suporterii, nu și jucătorii!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

Potrivit lui Grindeanu, avionul Nordis e de două ore la Otopeni şi chiar nu mai poate sta mult să îl aştepte. „Mă sună omul ăla că e pe avarii pe pistă de două ore […] The post Grindeanu cere să i se spună clar dacă va fi premier sau nu, că îl aşteaptă avionul Nordis pe avarii appeared first on Times New Roman .

Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

medical.title: A surprising brain discovery is forcing scientists to rethink movement disorders
medical.summary: A surprising discovery is overturning a long-held assumption about how the brain’s movement center works. Researchers found that two key cerebellar cell types—thought to be tightly linked—often don’t behave in predictable ways, even though one directly influences the other. The finding suggests scientists may have been relying on the wrong signals when studying disorders such as dystonia, ataxia, and tremor.

medical.title: Why 25 minutes may matter in music therapy sessions: Heart-rate synchrony peaks later
medical.summary: New research has discovered the time it takes for a therapist and patient to reach the moment of strongest connection during a therapy session—around 25 minutes. The study, published in the journal Frontiers in Psychology, is the first to identify how long it can take for heart-rate synchrony to peak during music therapy.

medical.title: Ways to make clinical trials more informative and reduce research waste
medical.summary: A major international study published in JAMA Network Open has identified practical actions that could improve the quality, usefulness and reliability of randomized clinical trials around the world. The research, conducted as part of the INFORM (Improving pre-funding peer review to increase the informativeness of randomized trials) project led by Professor Shaun Treweek, draws on interviews with 55 stakeholders from 16 countries across six continents, including trial investigators, funders, ethicists, regulators, industry representatives and sponsors.

medical.title: Exertion tests offer insight into recovery from youth concussions
medical.summary: Clinical exams that incorporate gait analysis after physical exertion may provide insight into how youths are recovering after concussions and help determine whether they are ready to return to sports, UT Southwestern Medical Center and Scottish Rite for Children (SRC) researchers found. Their study, published in the Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine, showed significant differences in the walking patterns and cognitive abilities of concussed youths after an experimental protocol.

medical.title: Wearable device reveals how sleep changes from freshman to senior year for college students
medical.summary: Sleep is a fundamental aspect of human health that plays a crucial role in cognitive function, academic performance and social interactions. A study published in PLOS One by Yao Zhao at Temple University, U.S., and colleagues suggests that wearable devices can provide accurate, longitudinal sleep data to facilitate analyses of changing sleep patterns over time.

medical.title: A simple message helps keep stem cell donors on track
medical.summary: When a patient needs a stem cell transplant, finding a registered donor is only the first step. Some potential donors drop out before confirmatory typing, reducing the pool from which doctors can choose. Researchers from Osaka University and collaborators tested whether a small change in wording could help more donors continue. This study is published in the Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization.

medical.title: CAR T cell therapy targets key mutation driving rare blood cancers
medical.summary: A new form of CAR T-cell therapy has been designed to find and destroy the cancer-driving stem cells responsible for a group of blood cancers known as myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs), while leaving healthy blood cells unharmed, according to new research led by UCL and University of Oxford researchers.

medical.title: Moms' responsiveness to their babies may predict later childhood psychiatric disorders
medical.summary: When mothers were slower to vocally respond to their 1-year-old children's vocalizations, the children were more likely to have been diagnosed with a psychiatric disorder by age 7, according to a study published in PLOS One by Bethany Stanley of the University of Glasgow in the U.K. and colleagues.

medical.title: Novel biomarker beats leading diagnostic blood test at predicting Alzheimer's progression
medical.summary: Small loops of genetic material may be strong indicators of imminent Alzheimer's disease (AD) symptoms. In a new study published in Nature Medicine, researchers showed that elevated levels of certain circular RNAs (circRNAs) in the blood nearly tripled patients' risk of developing symptoms, suggesting these molecules are more sensitive to symptom onset than traditional AD biomarkers.

medical.title: Many treatments help chronic back pain, but only in the short term
medical.summary: A large-scale analysis by an international research team, led by Bochum University of Applied Sciences, shows that nonsurgical treatments such as massage, exercise therapy or acupuncture for chronic back pain can alleviate symptoms—but usually only for a few weeks. Clinically significant improvements do not persist in the long term.

medical.title: Why does our mind wander? New study taps into the tricks of staying on task
medical.summary: You're sitting at your desk, writing a short story. You remain focused, but after several hours, you still can't see how to end it. So you go for a run, allowing your thoughts to run with you. Instead of stumbling on a rock, you fall on a solution.

medical.title: DNA damage can trigger neurons to self-destruct
medical.summary: Over the past decade, researchers at WashU Medicine have established that a molecule called SARM1 is a central trigger in the loss of axons, the vital wiring of the nervous system. Axon loss is characteristic of many neurodegenerative diseases, such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Parkinson's disease, glaucoma and peripheral neuropathies, including those caused by common chemotherapies.

medical.title: Modern neuroscience is rediscovering an idea Freud had 130 years ago
medical.summary: What if Sigmund Freud was onto something that modern neuroscience is only now beginning to explain? A new paper argues that today's leading theory of the brain—as a prediction machine constantly anticipating the world—closely mirrors ideas psychoanalysis has explored for more than a century.

medical.title: Scientists discover a surprising link between vitamin C and brain health
medical.summary: Could something as simple as vitamin C help support a healthier aging brain? In a study of more than 2,000 older adults in Japan, researchers found that people with lower vitamin C levels in their blood also tended to have less gray matter and weaker connections in a key brain network involved in memory, attention, and other cognitive functions.

medical.title: Melanoma's secret to cheating death has finally been revealed
medical.summary: Scientists have solved a long-standing mystery by discovering the missing genetic ingredient that helps melanoma cells become effectively immortal. The breakthrough could open the door to new treatments aimed at disrupting one of cancer's most important survival strategies.

medical.title: Scientists discover a completely different way to fight viruses
medical.summary: Researchers have uncovered an unexpected antiviral defense system in sea anemones that works very differently from the one humans use. The discovery suggests evolution developed multiple ways to combat viruses, challenging long-held ideas about how animal immune systems evolved.

medical.title: One injection reversed osteoarthritis in weeks
medical.summary: A Colorado research team has created experimental osteoarthritis treatments that appear to regenerate damaged joints rather than just relieve pain. In animal studies, a single injection restored arthritic joints to a healthy state within weeks, while a second therapy repaired cartilage and bone defects by harnessing the body’s own healing cells.

science.title: When parasites stop having sex, they may become less picky about their hosts
science.summary: A new evolutionary theory suggests that some asexual parasites may temporarily be able to infect a wider range of hosts as harmful genetic mutations build up. In a paper published in Trends in Parasitology, Cock van Oosterhout, a professor from the University of East Anglia (UEA), suggests that some parasites may become "generalists" not because they adapt successfully to many hosts, but because harmful mutations build up after they stop having sex.

science.title: Walkable, greener neighborhoods linked to better physical and mental health across the U.S.
science.summary: A new big-data analysis of the U.S. pinpoints how urban design aids the health of city residents—especially when cities provide walking opportunities, greenery and mixed-use streets with a blend of commercial and residential activity.

science.title: Nanotubes and nanosheets boost fast energy storage
science.summary: The growing use of renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, requires energy storage systems that can charge quickly, deliver power efficiently and remain stable over long-term use. Batteries can store large amounts of energy, but they often take longer to charge.

science.title: A new CRASH clock measures the chance of satellite collisions, and it's ticking down fast
science.summary: Imagine a piece of space debris the size of a hockey puck slams into a Starlink satellite at about 10 kilometers per second. The kinetic energy is equivalent to 2 kilograms of TNT, or a fully loaded semitruck traveling at 100 kilometers an hour.

science.title: DNA-based nanoswitch can flip in milliseconds and stay in one state for days without continuous forcing
science.summary: Scientists have engineered a nanoscale switch using DNA "origami." Inspired by macroscale mechanical switches, the device achieves long-term functionality without the continuous forcing mechanism that past versions required while remaining capable of fast switching. The paper is published in the journal Science Robotics.

science.title: One amino acid may signal the 'point of no return' in dying leaves
science.summary: Before a leaf dies, plants recover nutrients that the rest of the plant can reuse for growth and survival. Researchers at Umeå Plant Science Center have now identified a metabolic "point of no return" linked to the amino acid arginine. The study suggests that plants use arginine as a signal to determine whether recovery remains possible or whether cells should commit to death, a discovery that could eventually help improve crop resilience under environmental stress.

science.title: Why turning off screens is so hard for children—and four tips to make it easier
science.summary: The challenges and consequences surrounding children's screen use are a leading concern for UK families.

science.title: Researchers discover novel SRV2 envelope protein for efficient CAR immune cell production
science.summary: A Korean research team has developed a new viral vector technology that significantly improves the production efficiency of next-generation cell and gene therapies known as CAR immune cell therapies, which are designed to recognize and destroy cancer cells.

science.title: AI-generated debate replies outscore real politicians on authenticity and coherence
science.summary: AI-generated impersonations of political figures are judged by members of the public to be more authentic, relevant and coherent than the speakers' actual debate responses, according to a study appearing in PLOS One, written by Steffen Herbold of the University of Passau in Germany, and colleagues.

science.title: Primate brains might have evolved to 'catch up' with larger bodies, but then kept growing
science.summary: A new analysis supports the previously overlooked "brain lag" hypothesis—the idea that, in some primate lineages, the evolution of larger body size preceded the evolution of larger brain size—while also building on that hypothesis by suggesting that some lineages' brain sizes then continued to grow beyond an expected baseline. Robin Dunbar of the University of Oxford presents these findings in the open-access journal PLOS One.

science.title: Can AI plan for heat emergencies better than simple rules? It depends
science.summary: The thermometer reads 95°F (35°C) in Brooklyn, and vulnerable individuals need information to take appropriate action. New York City officials must gather facts quickly to provide updates on cooling centers, power outages and other details that could save lives. Are these details best gleaned from simple, low-complexity methods or AI-based tools?

science.title: Single-atom catalyst turns lignin into valuable chemicals with near-complete conversion
science.summary: Researchers at The University of Manchester and Hebei University of Technology have identified how a new class of catalyst can break down lignin into useful chemical building blocks, offering a more sustainable route to replace fossil-based materials.

science.title: Crystal-design principle reveals how competing molecular forces control structure, color and phase transitions
science.summary: Organic molecular crystals can respond to external stimuli such as heat, light, and mechanical force, making them attractive candidates for next-generation functional materials. However, predicting how multiple intermolecular interactions cooperate or compete to govern crystal behavior remains a major challenge.

science.title: The Milky Way’s weird gamma-ray glow may be dark matter after all
science.summary: A strange gamma-ray glow at the center of the Milky Way has long sparked debate over whether it comes from hidden neutron stars or elusive dark matter. By applying machine learning to more than a million simulated observations, researchers included photon energy data for the first time and reached a different conclusion than many earlier studies.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Exporturile americane de GNL către Europa au scăzut sub 50% pentru prima dată în aproape doi ani
mix_items.summary: Exporturile de gaz natural lichefiat (GNL) ale Statelor Unite către Europa au scăzut semnificativ în luna iunie, deoarece preţurile mai ridicate din Asia şi importurile record ale Egiptului au determinat traderii să redirecţioneze transporturile către pieţe mai profitabile, transmite Reuters.

mix_items.title: Val de căldură record în Anglia: temperaturi fără precedent și cea mai fierbinte lună iunie din istorie
mix_items.summary: Anglia a cunoscut cea mai călduroasă lună iunie din istoria înregistrărilor sale, cu mai multe zile marcate de temperaturi record, a anunţat miercuri agenţia britanică de meteorologie Met Office.

mix_items.title: 😂 Tamaș, revoltat de legea care permite berea pe stadion: „Pot să bea doar suporterii, nu și jucătorii!”
mix_items.summary: Fostul fotbalist, actual drojdier, Gabriel Tamaş acuză o mare nedreptate în noua lege a stadioanelor! Potrivit lui Tamaş, deşi e un lucru bun că berea va fi în sfârşit permisă la meciuri, e „absolut revoltător” că de aceasta se vor putea bucura doar suporterii din galerie, nu şi jucătorii de pe teren. „După ce că […] The post Tamaș, revoltat de legea care permite berea pe stadion: „Pot să bea doar suporterii, nu și jucătorii!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Val de căldură istoric în Statele Unite. Autoritățile avertizează că pot fi doborâte recorduri de temperatură
mix_items.summary: O mare parte din teritoriul Statelor Unite se confruntă începând de miercuri cu o căldură sufocantă, care ar putea să doboare mai multe recorduri de temperatură în zilele următoare şi să afecteze totodată Cupa Mondială de fotbal şi festivităţile ocazionate de cea de-a 250-a aniversare a acestei ţări.

mix_items.title: Școala de vară BookLand își deschide porțile în Campusul din Vulturești
mix_items.summary: În perioada 06 iulie – 04 septembrie 2026, Asociația BookLand organizează prima ediție a programului „Școala de vară BookLand”. De luni până vineri, zilnic între orele 09:00 și 16:00, copiii… Articolul Școala de vară BookLand își deschide porțile în Campusul din Vulturești apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Craiova: Redescoperă frumusețea lucrurilor mărunte la cea dea IX-a ediția a Festivalului Luminii, în Parcul Nicolae Romanescu
mix_items.summary: Organizația Națională Cercetaşii României – Centru Local “Cetatea Băniei”, Craiova, vă invită sâmbătă, 4 iulie 2026, incepând cu ora 17:00, în Parcul Nicolae Romanescu, Zona Foișorului Fanfarei, la o nouă… Articolul Craiova: Redescoperă frumusețea lucrurilor mărunte la cea dea IX-a ediția a Festivalului Luminii, în Parcul Nicolae Romanescu apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Omul PSD-ului până la capăt: Nicuşor Dan a dat azi Bacul la mate în locul lui Ciolacu
mix_items.summary: Dacă mai era vreo îndoială că preşedintele face jocurile PSD-ului, acesta tocmai a spulberat-o! Nicuşor Dan a fost văzut azi la un grup şcolar din Buzău, unde surse politice spun că ar fi dat examenul de Bacalaureat la matematică în locul lui Marcel Ciolacu. „Da, poate l-am ajutat pe Ciolacu dându-mă drept el la Bac, […] The post Omul PSD-ului până la capăt: Nicuşor Dan a dat azi Bacul la mate în locul lui Ciolacu appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: START EUROPAfest 2026. Ediția 33 se deschide cu “Royal Jazz Night”
mix_items.summary: București, 3 iulie 2026 – EUROPAfest dă startul celei de-a 33-a ediții printr-un concert de gală dedicat jazzului live de calitate: Opening Gala Concert – Royal Jazz Night, ora 20:00,… Articolul START EUROPAfest 2026. Ediția 33 se deschide cu “Royal Jazz Night” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: OAR a inaugurat pavilionul Sides of Interconnected Learning la Congresul Mondial al Arhitecților UIA 2026 Barcelona. Devenind interdependenți: arhitectura românească în dialog cu lumea
mix_items.summary: În perioada 29 iunie – 2 iulie 2026, Ordinul Arhitecților din România (OAR) participă la cea de-a XXIX-a ediție a Congresului a Congresului Mondial al Arhitecților organizat de Uniunea Internațională a Arhitecților (UIA), desfășurat la Barcelona, Capitală Mondială a Arhitecturii 2026. Prin prezența sa în cadrul expoziției congresului găzduite de Muzeul de design /… Articolul OAR a inaugurat pavilionul Sides of Interconnected Learning la Congresul Mondial al Arhitecților UIA 2026 Barcelona. Devenind interdependenți: arhitectura românească în dialog cu lumea apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Grindeanu cere să i se spună clar dacă va fi premier sau nu, că îl aşteaptă avionul Nordis pe avarii
mix_items.summary: În prag de vacanţă parlamentară, şeful PSD şi-a pierdut răbdarea şi cere să-i spunem clar dacă îl punem premier sau nu! Potrivit lui Grindeanu, avionul Nordis e de două ore la Otopeni şi chiar nu mai poate sta mult să îl aştepte. „Mă sună omul ăla că e pe avarii pe pistă de două ore […] The post Grindeanu cere să i se spună clar dacă va fi premier sau nu, că îl aşteaptă avionul Nordis pe avarii appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: When parasites stop having sex, they may become less picky about their hosts
mix_items.summary: A new evolutionary theory suggests that some asexual parasites may temporarily be able to infect a wider range of hosts as harmful genetic mutations build up. In a paper published in Trends in Parasitology, Cock van Oosterhout, a professor from the University of East Anglia (UEA), suggests that some parasites may become "generalists" not because they adapt successfully to many hosts, but because harmful mutations build up after they stop having sex.

mix_items.title: World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași.
mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... Articolul World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Walkable, greener neighborhoods linked to better physical and mental health across the U.S.
mix_items.summary: A new big-data analysis of the U.S. pinpoints how urban design aids the health of city residents—especially when cities provide walking opportunities, greenery and mixed-use streets with a blend of commercial and residential activity.

mix_items.summary: În România, mulți copii încă se tem să vorbească despre abuzurile prin care trec. Conform unui studiu al Fundației World... mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: A surprising brain discovery is forcing scientists to rethink movement disorders
mix_items.summary: A surprising discovery is overturning a long-held assumption about how the brain’s movement center works. Researchers found that two key cerebellar cell types—thought to be tightly linked—often don’t behave in predictable ways, even though one directly influences the other. The finding suggests scientists may have been relying on the wrong signals when studying disorders such as dystonia, ataxia, and tremor.

mix_items.summary: Locație: București (Satul SOS) Nivel carieră: Mid-level (3-5 ani experiență) Departament: Financiar / Contabilitate Tip job: Full-time Cine suntem noi? La SOS Satele Copiilor România, oferim o familie copiilor ajunși... The post Contabil appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Why 25 minutes may matter in music therapy sessions: Heart-rate synchrony peaks later
mix_items.summary: New research has discovered the time it takes for a therapist and patient to reach the moment of strongest connection during a therapy session—around 25 minutes. The study, published in the journal Frontiers in Psychology, is the first to identify how long it can take for heart-rate synchrony to peak during music therapy.

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Nanotubes and nanosheets boost fast energy storage
mix_items.summary: The growing use of renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, requires energy storage systems that can charge quickly, deliver power efficiently and remain stable over long-term use. Batteries can store large amounts of energy, but they often take longer to charge.

mix_items.title: A new CRASH clock measures the chance of satellite collisions, and it's ticking down fast
mix_items.summary: Imagine a piece of space debris the size of a hockey puck slams into a Starlink satellite at about 10 kilometers per second. The kinetic energy is equivalent to 2 kilograms of TNT, or a fully loaded semitruck traveling at 100 kilometers an hour.

mix_items.title: DNA-based nanoswitch can flip in milliseconds and stay in one state for days without continuous forcing
mix_items.summary: Scientists have engineered a nanoscale switch using DNA "origami." Inspired by macroscale mechanical switches, the device achieves long-term functionality without the continuous forcing mechanism that past versions required while remaining capable of fast switching. The paper is published in the journal Science Robotics.

mix_items.title: Ways to make clinical trials more informative and reduce research waste
mix_items.summary: A major international study published in JAMA Network Open has identified practical actions that could improve the quality, usefulness and reliability of randomized clinical trials around the world. The research, conducted as part of the INFORM (Improving pre-funding peer review to increase the informativeness of randomized trials) project led by Professor Shaun Treweek, draws on interviews with 55 stakeholders from 16 countries across six continents, including trial investigators, funders, ethicists, regulators, industry representatives and sponsors.

mix_items.title: One amino acid may signal the 'point of no return' in dying leaves
mix_items.summary: Before a leaf dies, plants recover nutrients that the rest of the plant can reuse for growth and survival. Researchers at Umeå Plant Science Center have now identified a metabolic "point of no return" linked to the amino acid arginine. The study suggests that plants use arginine as a signal to determine whether recovery remains possible or whether cells should commit to death, a discovery that could eventually help improve crop resilience under environmental stress.

mix_items.title: Exertion tests offer insight into recovery from youth concussions
mix_items.summary: Clinical exams that incorporate gait analysis after physical exertion may provide insight into how youths are recovering after concussions and help determine whether they are ready to return to sports, UT Southwestern Medical Center and Scottish Rite for Children (SRC) researchers found. Their study, published in the Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine, showed significant differences in the walking patterns and cognitive abilities of concussed youths after an experimental protocol.

mix_items.title: Why turning off screens is so hard for children—and four tips to make it easier
mix_items.summary: The challenges and consequences surrounding children's screen use are a leading concern for UK families.

mix_items.title: Researchers discover novel SRV2 envelope protein for efficient CAR immune cell production
mix_items.summary: A Korean research team has developed a new viral vector technology that significantly improves the production efficiency of next-generation cell and gene therapies known as CAR immune cell therapies, which are designed to recognize and destroy cancer cells.

mix_items.title: Wearable device reveals how sleep changes from freshman to senior year for college students
mix_items.summary: Sleep is a fundamental aspect of human health that plays a crucial role in cognitive function, academic performance and social interactions. A study published in PLOS One by Yao Zhao at Temple University, U.S., and colleagues suggests that wearable devices can provide accurate, longitudinal sleep data to facilitate analyses of changing sleep patterns over time.

mix_items.title: AI-generated debate replies outscore real politicians on authenticity and coherence
mix_items.summary: AI-generated impersonations of political figures are judged by members of the public to be more authentic, relevant and coherent than the speakers' actual debate responses, according to a study appearing in PLOS One, written by Steffen Herbold of the University of Passau in Germany, and colleagues.

mix_items.title: A simple message helps keep stem cell donors on track
mix_items.summary: When a patient needs a stem cell transplant, finding a registered donor is only the first step. Some potential donors drop out before confirmatory typing, reducing the pool from which doctors can choose. Researchers from Osaka University and collaborators tested whether a small change in wording could help more donors continue. This study is published in the Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization.

mix_items.title: Primate brains might have evolved to 'catch up' with larger bodies, but then kept growing
mix_items.summary: A new analysis supports the previously overlooked "brain lag" hypothesis—the idea that, in some primate lineages, the evolution of larger body size preceded the evolution of larger brain size—while also building on that hypothesis by suggesting that some lineages' brain sizes then continued to grow beyond an expected baseline. Robin Dunbar of the University of Oxford presents these findings in the open-access journal PLOS One.

mix_items.title: CAR T cell therapy targets key mutation driving rare blood cancers
mix_items.summary: A new form of CAR T-cell therapy has been designed to find and destroy the cancer-driving stem cells responsible for a group of blood cancers known as myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs), while leaving healthy blood cells unharmed, according to new research led by UCL and University of Oxford researchers.

mix_items.title: Moms' responsiveness to their babies may predict later childhood psychiatric disorders
mix_items.summary: When mothers were slower to vocally respond to their 1-year-old children's vocalizations, the children were more likely to have been diagnosed with a psychiatric disorder by age 7, according to a study published in PLOS One by Bethany Stanley of the University of Glasgow in the U.K. and colleagues.

mix_items.title: Can AI plan for heat emergencies better than simple rules? It depends
mix_items.summary: The thermometer reads 95°F (35°C) in Brooklyn, and vulnerable individuals need information to take appropriate action. New York City officials must gather facts quickly to provide updates on cooling centers, power outages and other details that could save lives. Are these details best gleaned from simple, low-complexity methods or AI-based tools?

mix_items.title: Single-atom catalyst turns lignin into valuable chemicals with near-complete conversion
mix_items.summary: Researchers at The University of Manchester and Hebei University of Technology have identified how a new class of catalyst can break down lignin into useful chemical building blocks, offering a more sustainable route to replace fossil-based materials.

mix_items.title: Novel biomarker beats leading diagnostic blood test at predicting Alzheimer's progression
mix_items.summary: Small loops of genetic material may be strong indicators of imminent Alzheimer's disease (AD) symptoms. In a new study published in Nature Medicine, researchers showed that elevated levels of certain circular RNAs (circRNAs) in the blood nearly tripled patients' risk of developing symptoms, suggesting these molecules are more sensitive to symptom onset than traditional AD biomarkers.

mix_items.title: Crystal-design principle reveals how competing molecular forces control structure, color and phase transitions
mix_items.summary: Organic molecular crystals can respond to external stimuli such as heat, light, and mechanical force, making them attractive candidates for next-generation functional materials. However, predicting how multiple intermolecular interactions cooperate or compete to govern crystal behavior remains a major challenge.

mix_items.title: Many treatments help chronic back pain, but only in the short term
mix_items.summary: A large-scale analysis by an international research team, led by Bochum University of Applied Sciences, shows that nonsurgical treatments such as massage, exercise therapy or acupuncture for chronic back pain can alleviate symptoms—but usually only for a few weeks. Clinically significant improvements do not persist in the long term.

mix_items.title: Why does our mind wander? New study taps into the tricks of staying on task
mix_items.summary: You're sitting at your desk, writing a short story. You remain focused, but after several hours, you still can't see how to end it. So you go for a run, allowing your thoughts to run with you. Instead of stumbling on a rock, you fall on a solution.

mix_items.title: DNA damage can trigger neurons to self-destruct
mix_items.summary: Over the past decade, researchers at WashU Medicine have established that a molecule called SARM1 is a central trigger in the loss of axons, the vital wiring of the nervous system. Axon loss is characteristic of many neurodegenerative diseases, such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Parkinson's disease, glaucoma and peripheral neuropathies, including those caused by common chemotherapies.

mix_items.title: Modern neuroscience is rediscovering an idea Freud had 130 years ago
mix_items.summary: What if Sigmund Freud was onto something that modern neuroscience is only now beginning to explain? A new paper argues that today's leading theory of the brain—as a prediction machine constantly anticipating the world—closely mirrors ideas psychoanalysis has explored for more than a century.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a surprising link between vitamin C and brain health
mix_items.summary: Could something as simple as vitamin C help support a healthier aging brain? In a study of more than 2,000 older adults in Japan, researchers found that people with lower vitamin C levels in their blood also tended to have less gray matter and weaker connections in a key brain network involved in memory, attention, and other cognitive functions.

mix_items.title: The Milky Way’s weird gamma-ray glow may be dark matter after all
mix_items.summary: A strange gamma-ray glow at the center of the Milky Way has long sparked debate over whether it comes from hidden neutron stars or elusive dark matter. By applying machine learning to more than a million simulated observations, researchers included photon energy data for the first time and reached a different conclusion than many earlier studies.

mix_items.title: Melanoma's secret to cheating death has finally been revealed
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved a long-standing mystery by discovering the missing genetic ingredient that helps melanoma cells become effectively immortal. The breakthrough could open the door to new treatments aimed at disrupting one of cancer's most important survival strategies.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a completely different way to fight viruses
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered an unexpected antiviral defense system in sea anemones that works very differently from the one humans use. The discovery suggests evolution developed multiple ways to combat viruses, challenging long-held ideas about how animal immune systems evolved.

mix_items.title: One injection reversed osteoarthritis in weeks
mix_items.summary: A Colorado research team has created experimental osteoarthritis treatments that appear to regenerate damaged joints rather than just relieve pain. In animal studies, a single injection restored arthritic joints to a healthy state within weeks, while a second therapy repaired cartilage and bone defects by harnessing the body’s own healing cells.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Am incercat sa fiu spontan. Mi-am programat pentru saptamana viitoare.

satire_ro.title: Potop şi trăsnete în Bucureşti după ce Anca Alexandrescu l-a trădat pe trimisul lui Dumnezeu
